question_title: Will Donald Trump publicly threaten to raise tariffs on China between May 17 and May 31, 2026?
summary: The question asks whether Trump will publicly threaten to raise tariffs on China within 15 days (May 17-31, 2026), of which 5 have already elapsed without any qualifying threat. The post-Beijing summit (May 14-15) created strong conciliatory momentum with deal announcements and warm bilateral rhetoric. Historical post-deal quiet periods all lasted >47 days, and we're only 6 days post-summit. Quantitative models conditioned on this conciliation period estimate 6-15% probability. However, the resolution criteria are deliberately broad - conditional threats count, and Trump's rhetorical style naturally includes "if they don't follow through, tariffs will go up" framing even in positive contexts. The Iran-China arms issue provides a ready trigger (April 2026 precedent), and the vague deal terms could unravel. Adjusting upward from the model range for definition breadth and Trump's verbal patterns yields approximately 25%.

factors.description: Post-summit conciliatory tone strongly suppresses threats - all 3 historical post-deal quiet periods lasted >47 days (98, 112, 47), and we're only 6 days post-summit with 10 days remaining
Active deal-touting (Boeing 200 jets, $17B beef, soybeans, rare earths) and new bilateral trade boards create strong diplomatic incentive to maintain positive tone
Trump's tariff aggression redirected at EU (25% auto tariffs, July 4 deadline) - China is not the current target
SCOTUS struck down IEEPA tariffs (Feb 2026), limiting legal basis for credible new China tariff threats; Section 122 authority also under challenge

Broad resolution definition - conditional threats count ('if China does X, tariffs go up'), and Trump naturally uses this framing even when celebrating deals. 'May impose' and 'considering' also qualify.
Trump's general volatility and high base rate of China tariff rhetoric: ~20 distinct threats in 17 months, averaging one every 25 days
Iran-China arms relationship is unresolved and produced a 50% tariff threat on April 8, 2026 - any new intelligence could instantly trigger a threat
Post-summit deals are vague ('big promises, thin results' per Politico) and could publicly unravel, providing grounds for tariff threats
10 remaining days includes multiple press conferences, possible rallies, Truth Social posts, and interviews where offhand qualifying remarks are possible

reasoning:
## Forecast Summary

**Will Donald Trump publicly threaten to raise tariffs on China between May 17 and May 31, 2026?**

**Probability: 25%**

### Key Reasoning

**Status quo favors NO:** The post-Beijing summit period (May 14-15) established strong conciliatory momentum. In the first 5 days of the window (May 17-21), no qualifying threat was found — instead, the period featured active deal announcements (Boeing 200 jets, $17B beef, soybeans, rare earths) and warm bilateral rhetoric. All 3 historical post-deal quiet periods lasted >47 days, and we're only 6 days post-summit.

**But the definition is broad:** Conditional threats count ("if China does X, I will raise tariffs"), and Trump naturally uses this framing. Over 10 remaining days of press appearances, rallies, and social media, even offhand qualifying remarks could trigger resolution.

**Key risk factors:**
- Iran-China arms relationship (produced a 50% tariff threat in April 2026)
- Vague summit deals could publicly unravel
- Trump's high base rate (~20 China tariff threats in 17 months)

**Protective factors:**
- Strong post-summit diplomatic momentum
- Active deal celebration mode
- Tariff aggression redirected at EU
- Legal constraints post-SCOTUS ruling
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will Donald Trump publicly threaten to raise tariffs on China between May 17 and May 31, 2026?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43555)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if, between 00:00 UTC on May 17, 2026 and 23:59 UTC on May 31, 2026, Donald Trump publicly states, in his capacity as U.S. President, that he is considering, will, or may impose new or higher tariffs on imports from China (or on any specific category of Chinese goods) above the levels in effect as of May 17, 2026. The statement must be made by Trump personally — in a Truth Social post on his @realDonaldTrump account, an on-camera remark, a press conference, a speech, an interview, or a signed executive order/proclamation — and must be reported by [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions). Conditional threats (e.g., 'if China does X, I will raise tariffs') count. Statements by other administration officials (e.g., the USTR, Treasury Secretary, Commerce Secretary) do NOT count unless Trump himself reiterates the threat. Statements merely defending or describing existing tariffs, without threatening any increase, do NOT count.
fine_print: If the threat is delivered in a written executive order, proclamation, or social media post, the timestamp of publication governs. If delivered orally, the date/time of the remark as reported governs. Threats to impose tariffs on third countries for reasons related to China (e.g., on transshipment hubs) do NOT count unless Trump explicitly names China or Chinese goods as the target. If Trump dies, resigns, or is otherwise no longer President during the window, only statements made while he was President count.

factors.description: Post-summit conciliatory tone strongly suppresses threats - all historical post-deal quiet periods lasted >47 days, and we're only 6 days post-summit
Active deal-touting and bilateral trade boards create diplomatic incentive to avoid threats
Trump's tariff aggression redirected at EU (25% auto tariffs), reducing attention on China
SCOTUS struck down IEEPA tariffs, limiting legal basis for credible new threats
Broad definition - conditional threats count ('if China does X, tariffs go up'), Trump naturally uses this framing even in positive contexts
Trump's general volatility and pattern of ~20 China tariff threats in 17 months
Iran-China arms relationship is unresolved; any new intelligence could trigger immediate tariff threat (April 2026 precedent)
Post-summit deals are vague ('big promises, thin results') and could unravel, triggering threats
10 remaining days includes many press opportunities where offhand qualifying remarks are possible

input.anchor: Unconditional base rate: ~45% (20 China tariff threats in 510 days, 15-day window). Conditional on post-summit conciliation: ~15% (historical quiet periods all > 47 days).
input.assessment:
The post-summit period creates strong suppression of tariff threats. In all 3 historical post-deal/summit periods (Geneva, Busan, SCOTUS), quiet periods lasted 47-112 days. The 16-day window from summit to end of question is well within the safe zone historically.

However, the broad definition of qualifying threats (conditional threats count) and Trump's verbal style mean that offhand remarks during press conferences, rallies, or interviews could technically qualify. Trump frequently frames deals with implicit or explicit consequences for non-compliance.

The main risk factors are: (1) Iran-China arms situation providing a ready trigger, (2) vague deal details creating grounds for claiming non-compliance, (3) Trump's natural inclination toward conditional language about tariffs. But against this: diplomatic momentum, active deal celebration, EU focus, and legal constraints.

Models range from 1% (Weibull quiet period) to 22% (unconditional exponential), with post-summit adjustment bringing most to 6-15%. Adding qualitative adjustments for breadth of definition: ~25%.

Key tension: the historical pattern says quiet periods are very durable in their early weeks, but Trump's rhetorical style means technically qualifying statements could emerge at any time.
input.calibration_notes: The 25% estimate is above most model outputs (6-15% range for post-summit scenarios) but I'm adjusting upward for the broad definition of qualifying threats. Historical post-summit periods all lasted >47 days without a clear threat, which would argue for <10%. But conditional threats are different from deliberate escalation threats - they're much more common in Trump's rhetoric.
input.key_uncertainties: 1. How strictly will resolution be interpreted? A casual 'and if they don't follow through, there will be consequences' at a press conference could qualify or not depending on exact wording. 2. Whether Iran-China intelligence triggers a specific threat. 3. Whether any trade deal details publicly collapse before May 31.
input.tool_audit: Research tool: Excellent - two parallel queries returned comprehensive, source-backed findings confirming no threats in May 17-21. Coherence links: Empty but expected for new question. Code execution: Useful for modeling quiet period distributions. All tools functioned well.
input.update_triggers: 1. Any breaking news about China-Iran arms transfers. 2. Reports that China is backing away from summit commitments. 3. Trump rally or press conference with Q&A about China trade. 4. Taiwan military provocation by PLA.
input.process_reflection: The research tool was very effective for this question - the Opus sub-agent found comprehensive coverage of the post-summit period and clearly identified that no threats were made in May 17-21. The quantitative modeling in sandbox was helpful for establishing a range. The main challenge was calibrating between the historical quiet-period base rate (very low) and the broadened definition of qualifying threats. The question design cleverly exploits this tension.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Systematic Review

### Resolution Criteria — PASS
Resolution criteria are clearly provided and properly recovered via `get_metaculus_questions`. The agent correctly engages with specific terms: conditional threats count, must be Trump personally, must threaten NEW or HIGHER tariffs (not merely defending existing tariffs). Factors 1 and 5 explicitly reference these criteria.

### Hallucinated Evidence — None Found

**Factor 1 (quiet periods >47 days):** The trace's research entry confirms "Post-deal pauses typically lasted 5-11 weeks." The agent's code models three specific quiet periods: post-Geneva (98 days), post-Busan (112 days), post-SCOTUS (47 days). These are derived from the research data points. The research confirms the most recent threat was April 8, 2026, and the Geneva period was ~14 weeks (98 days). The 47-day post-SCOTUS gap (Feb 20 to Apr 8) is arithmetically correct. Grounded.

**Factor 3 (EU tariff redirection):** Trace confirms: "Trump DID threaten 25% auto tariffs on the EU during this same period (May 19-21), but NOT on China" (France24, multiple outlets). Grounded.

**Factor 4 (SCOTUS struck down IEEPA tariffs):** Trace confirms via NPR: "Supreme Court Strikes Down Most Trump Tariffs" on Feb 20, 2026. Hinrich Foundation confirms Section 122 legal challenges. Grounded.

**Factor 6 (20 China tariff threats in 17 months):** Research data point confirms "Total distinct China tariff threats/actions (Jan 2025-May 2026): 20 events." Grounded.

**Factor 7 (Iran-China arms, April 2026 precedent):** Trace confirms CNBC reported Trump threatened 50% tariffs on China over Iran arms shipments on April 8, 2026. Confirmed "NOT repeated post-summit." Grounded.

### Double-Counting — Minor, Not Material
Factors 1 and 2 partially overlap (both relate to post-summit diplomatic environment), but Factor 1 is statistical (quiet period pattern) while Factor 2 is about specific diplomatic incentive structure (deal-touting, trade boards). Their combined logit impact (-2.3) is significant but the agent's final probability (25%) sits well above what the factor-implied probability would be, suggesting the agent is not naively summing factors. Not flagging.

### Wrong-Direction Factors — PASS
All signs correct. Factors 1-4 (negative logits) push toward NO (no threat). Factors 5-9 (positive logits) push toward YES (threat likely). Signs match descriptions.

### Contradictory Assessment — PASS
Assessment concludes 25%, consistent with strong suppression from post-summit period (net negative factors dominate) but adjusted upward for breadth of qualifying statements. The factor-implied probability (~30.3% per the agent's reflection) and the 25% final estimate are within 5.3pp — noted and properly reconciled in the reflection step.

### Pre-Publication Event — PASS
Question window starts May 17, published May 21. Agent correctly treats the May 17-21 period as elapsed (no qualifying threats found), reducing the remaining window to 10 days. This is used for forward-looking probability estimation, not as a pre-existing resolution claim. Handled correctly.

### Weak Counterargument — PASS
The counterargument is substantive: (1) Trump's rhetorical style makes conditional threats nearly unavoidable in any 10-day window, (2) resolution criteria are deliberately broad ("may impose" counts), (3) Iran-China is a live unresolved trigger with April precedent, (4) post-summit honeymoon periods may be shorter in 2nd term, (5) the small n=3 historical quiet-period sample is thin. This constructs a coherent alternative story.

### Overconfident Self-Assessment — PASS
Confidence at 0.45 is appropriate. Models range from 1% to 22%, qualitative adjustments add substantial uncertainty, the historical quiet-period sample is only n=3, and the broad definition creates genuine ambiguity about what counts as a qualifying statement.

### Probability Assessment — PASS
Quantitative models cluster at 7-22% when conditioned on post-summit context. The agent adds ~10-15pp for the breadth of qualifying statements (conditional threats count) and other qualitative factors, arriving at 25%. My independent estimate: 15-25%. The 25% sits at the upper edge of my range but is defensible given: (1) the resolution criteria's breadth, (2) Trump's natural conditional language about tariffs, (3) the Iran-China wild card, and (4) the thin n=3 sample for quiet periods. Not meaningfully divergent from my assessment.

### Anchor Divergence — PASS
The agent identifies two anchors: unconditional ~45% and conditional on post-summit ~15%. The final 25% is 10pp above the conditional anchor, which is well-explained by the breadth-of-definition adjustment (Factor 5, logit=+0.8). The departure is justified by a specific, well-articulated factor.

### Minor Note (Not Flagging)
The research says "Post-deal pauses typically lasted 5-11 weeks" (35-77 days), while Factor 1 claims "all historical post-deal quiet periods lasted >47 days." The 5-week lower bound (35 days) suggests some quiet periods might have been shorter than 47 days. However, the agent's three specifically measured quiet periods (98, 112, 47 days) are all ≥47 days, and the "5-11 weeks" characterization may be a general range rather than an enumeration of the same three periods. This is a minor tension that doesn't change the forecast direction or magnitude.

### Tool Audit — PASS
Agent reports "7 calls, 0 errors." Ground truth confirms 7 calls, 0 errors (0.0% error rate). No discrepancies.

### Worldview Consistency — PASS
No contradictory worldview entries found. The archived Iran-war and China-Taiwan entries are stale (from early May with no web access), and don't contradict any current claims. No numeric sub-forecast exists for binary-numeric consistency checks.

## Summary
No material errors found. All factual claims in the factors are grounded in the research trace. The 25% probability estimate is within the reasonable range given the evidence — perhaps slightly high but defensibly so given the breadth of qualifying statements under the resolution criteria.
